title: A Memory (From A Sonnet-Sequence)
content:
Somewhile before the dawn I rose, and stept
    Softly along the dim way to your room,
    And found you sleeping in the quiet gloom,
    And holiness about you as you slept.
    I knelt there; till your waking fingers crept
    About my head, and held it. I had rest
    Unhoped this side of Heaven, beneath your breast.
    I knelt a long time, still; nor even wept.

    It was great wrong you did me; and for gain
    Of that poor moment's kindliness, and ease,
    And sleepy mother-comfort!
     Child, you know
    How easily love leaps out to dreams like these,
    Who has seen them true. And love that's wakened so
    Takes all too long to lay asleep again.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Rupert Brooke delivers a powerful performance in "A Memory (From A Sonnet-Sequence)"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
